---

**Yes — C3 will appear before C1** in `git log --date-order` output, despite being C1's child and having an earlier timestamp.

## Why

The key is what `--date-order` actually guarantees. From the git man page:

> **`--date-order`**: Show no parents before all of its children are shown, but otherwise show commits in the commit timestamp order.

It shares the same topological invariant as `--topo-order`: **a parent commit cannot be shown until all its reachable children have been shown first.** The difference is only that ties are broken by timestamp.

## Walking through your specific DAG

```
C4 (10:10)  ← merge
├── C2 (10:05)
│   └── C1 (10:00)
└── C3 (09:55)  ← child of C1 with EARLIER timestamp than C1
    └── C1 (10:00)
```

Git starts at C4 and applies the rule. C1 is parent of both C2 and C3, so **C1 cannot appear until both C2 and C3 have been output**.

| Step | Eligible commits | Chosen (latest date) | Output |
|------|-----------------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | C4 (no unshown children) | C4 | C4 |
| 2 | C2, C3 (C4 shown) | C2 (10:05 > 09:55) | C2 |
| 3 | C3 (C4 shown) | C3 | C3 |
| 4 | C1 (both C2 and C3 shown) | C1 | C1 |

**Final order: C4 → C2 → C3 → C1**

## The core insight

C3 appears before C1 **not** because of its timestamp, but because the topological constraint forces C1 to wait. Git's `--date-order` is not a simple sort — it's a topologically-constrained traversal where timestamps only control ordering *among eligible commits at the same level*.

This is the legitimate reason timestamps can "invert" in `git log --date-order`: if a commit has an earlier author date than its parent, it will still appear first in the output because the parent can't be shown until all children are exhausted. Developer clock skew (your C3 was committed on a machine with a slow clock, or was rebased/cherry-picked) is the common real-world cause.
